---
title: "Counting: Permutations and Combinations"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Probability Foundations"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from math import comb, perm, factorial

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

POINT  = "#2b3a67"     # data / counts
LINE   = "#c1121f"     # the curve / theoretical value
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights
GREY   = "#6c757d"
FILL   = "#9fb3d1"     # shaded areas / bars
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This article opens the **distribution catalog** —
> the stretch of the Probability track (PROB-10 through PROB-16) that names and derives
> the workhorse distributions every downstream track consumes. Before we can write down
> the binomial pmf in [PROB-11 *Bernoulli, Binomial & Negative-Binomial*], we need to
> count: when $n$ coin flips are equally likely, *how many* of the $2^n$ outcome
> sequences have exactly $k$ heads? That number is a **combination**, and combinations
> (with their cousins, permutations) are the subject here. The only thing this article
> assumes is the equally-likely / classical interpretation of probability built in
> [PROB-00 *What Is Probability?*] — that when an experiment has $N$ equally likely
> outcomes and an event $A$ contains $|A|$ of them, $P(A) = |A|/N$. Everything probability
> needs to *count* the numerator and denominator of that ratio is built here from one
> principle. This article **sets up** [PROB-11] (the binomial coefficient *is* the
> normalizing count of the binomial pmf, and the binomial theorem is what makes that pmf
> sum to one), [PROB-23 *Order Statistics*] (whose $k$-th-smallest-value density rests on
> exactly the multinomial count we build in @sec-multinomial), and — across tracks — the
> permutation test of Statistical Inference E3, whose null distribution is a uniform
> draw over the $n!$ relabelings we count in @sec-permutations.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

When every outcome of an experiment is equally likely, a probability is a *ratio of
counts* — favorable outcomes over total outcomes — and computing it reduces to counting
without listing. This article builds the entire machinery from a single seed, the
**multiplication principle**: if one choice can be made $a$ ways and a second
(independently) $b$ ways, the pair can be made $ab$ ways. From it we derive, with every
step shown, the **factorial** $n!$ (arrangements of $n$ distinct objects), the
**permutation** count $P(n,k) = n!/(n-k)!$ (ordered selections of $k$ from $n$), and the
**combination** count $\binom{n}{k} = n!/[k!\,(n-k)!]$ (unordered selections). We prove
the binomial coefficient's symmetry and **Pascal's recurrence**, use them to prove the
**binomial theorem**, and organize the whole subject into the **2×2 table** of
sampling — ordered vs. unordered, with vs. without replacement — so that any counting
problem you meet can be classified and solved. The single claim the article defends is
that **all of elementary counting is one principle applied carefully**, and that knowing
*which cell of the 2×2 table you are in* is the entire skill.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Counting is multiplication done carefully. From the **multiplication principle** — $a$
independent choices times $b$ independent choices give $ab$ outcomes — everything else
follows. **Order matters** ⟹ you are counting *permutations*, $P(n,k) = n!/(n-k)!$.
**Order does not matter** ⟹ you are counting *combinations*,
$\binom{n}{k} = \frac{n!}{k!\,(n-k)!}$, which is the permutation count divided by $k!$ to
erase the orderings you double-counted. The binomial coefficient $\binom{n}{k}$ is the
number of equally-likely outcomes a probability will count, and it is the bridge to the
binomial distribution in [PROB-11].
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The one formula to carry in your head is
$\binom{n}{k} = P(n,k)/k!$: *count the ordered selections, then divide out the orderings
you do not care about.* Permutations and combinations are not two unrelated formulas to
memorize — combinations are permutations with the order quotiented away, and seeing that
quotient is the difference between reciting the formula and understanding it.

# Why we are doing this: probability as a ratio of counts {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **when outcomes are equally likely, a
probability is a fraction of outcomes — so how do we count the top and the bottom of that
fraction without writing every outcome down?** Every formula below is an answer to some
piece of that question. If a step ever feels like an arbitrary rule, return here.

## The original problem

The mathematics of counting was forced into existence by gambling. In the summer of 1654,
a French nobleman and compulsive gambler, the Chevalier de Méré, posed a puzzle to Blaise
Pascal about how the stakes of an interrupted game of chance should be divided fairly. The
correspondence that followed between Pascal and Pierre de Fermat is usually marked as the
birth of probability theory — and the very first thing they had to do was *count*. To work
out the chance of an event, they reasoned that all the elementary outcomes of the dice were
equally likely, so the probability of an event was simply the number of outcomes favorable
to it divided by the total number of outcomes. The whole problem then collapsed to a
counting problem: *how many* ways can the game finish, and *how many* of those ways favor
each player?

That move — **probability = favorable count / total count, when outcomes are equally
likely** — is exactly the classical interpretation made precise in [PROB-00]. It is the
oldest and most concrete way to put a number on uncertainty, and it works whenever a
physical symmetry (a fair die, a well-shuffled deck, a randomly assigned treatment) makes
all outcomes interchangeable. But it is useless until you can count *large* collections
without enumerating them. Nobody can list the $\binom{52}{5} = 2{,}598{,}960$ poker hands
by hand. The science of counting-without-listing — **combinatorics** — is what makes the
classical definition usable, and that is why a probability track must build it before it
can build a single distribution.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is a probability actually a ratio of counts, and when is that the wrong model?"*
Only when the outcomes are genuinely **equally likely** — a physical or designed symmetry
guarantees it (fair dice, a shuffled deck, a simple random sample, a random permutation of
treatment labels). If the outcomes are *not* equally likely (a loaded die, words in
English text, customers arriving at a store), counting outcomes gives you the wrong answer;
you need a probability *model* on the outcomes instead, which is what the rest of the
catalog (PROB-11 onward) supplies. The counting in this article computes the *size* of
events; turning sizes into probabilities is honest **only under equal likelihood**. Keep
that caveat sharp — it is the single most common misuse, and we return to it in
@sec-failure.
:::

# Intuition before symbols: the choice tree {#sec-intuition}

Before any factorials, picture how a sequence of choices multiplies. Suppose you are
building an outcome one decision at a time. The first decision has some number of branches;
from the end of *each* of those branches, the second decision fans out again; and so on.
The total number of complete paths through this **choice tree** is the product of the
branch counts — not their sum — because every first-choice branch carries a full copy of
the second-choice fan beneath it.

@fig-tree draws this for a tiny case: choosing an outcome in two stages, $3$ ways then $2$
ways, giving $3 \times 2 = 6$ leaves. The picture is the entire proof of the multiplication
principle; the formula is just the picture written in symbols.

```{python}
#| label: fig-tree
#| fig-cap: 'The choice tree behind the multiplication principle. A first choice with $3$ branches, each followed by a second choice with $2$ branches, produces $3 \times 2 = 6$ complete paths (leaves). The total is a *product* because every one of the $3$ first-stage branches carries its own full copy of the $2$ second-stage branches. Counting is multiplication done carefully.'
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.2))

root = (0.0, 0.5)
first = [(0.33, y) for y in (0.85, 0.5, 0.15)]
labels1 = ["A", "B", "C"]
leaf_ys = []
for (fx, fy), lab in zip(first, labels1):
    ax.plot([root[0], fx], [root[1], fy], color=GREY, lw=1.4)
    ax.scatter([fx], [fy], color=POINT, s=70, zorder=3)
    ax.text(fx - 0.015, fy + 0.045, lab, fontsize=11, ha="center", color=POINT)
    for dy in (+0.08, -0.08):
        ly = fy + dy
        ax.plot([fx, 0.72], [fy, ly], color=GREY, lw=1.2)
        ax.scatter([0.72], [ly], color=ACCENT, s=55, zorder=3)
        leaf_ys.append(ly)

ax.scatter([root[0]], [root[1]], color=LINE, s=110, zorder=4)
ax.text(root[0] - 0.03, root[1], "start", fontsize=11, ha="right", va="center", color=LINE)
for i, ly in enumerate(sorted(leaf_ys, reverse=True), start=1):
    ax.text(0.78, ly, f"outcome {i}", fontsize=9.5, va="center", color=ACCENT)

ax.text(0.165, 0.97, "choice 1:  3 ways", fontsize=10.5, ha="center", color=POINT)
ax.text(0.525, 0.97, "choice 2:  2 ways each", fontsize=10.5, ha="center", color=ACCENT)
ax.text(0.5, 0.02, r"total $= 3 \times 2 = 6$ outcomes", fontsize=12, ha="center")
ax.set_xlim(-0.12, 0.98); ax.set_ylim(0.0, 1.02)
ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
```

The whole of this article is that tree, specialized. When the branch counts shrink by one
at each stage ($n$, then $n-1$, then $n-2$, …) because we are using up distinct objects, the
product becomes a factorial and we are counting **arrangements**. When we then decide we do
not care about the order in which we made the choices, we divide out the redundant paths and
we are counting **selections**. Hold the tree in mind; the symbols will never feel arbitrary
if you do.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## The multiplication principle {#sec-mult}

Everything starts here. We state it for two stages, prove it, then extend it to any number
of stages by the same argument.

::: {#thm-mult}
**Theorem (multiplication principle).** Suppose a procedure consists of two stages. If the
first stage can occur in $a$ ways, and *for each* of those ways the second stage can occur
in $b$ ways, then the whole procedure can occur in $a \cdot b$ ways. More generally, if a
procedure has $r$ stages with $n_1, n_2, \dots, n_r$ ways at each stage (the count at each
stage not depending on *which* earlier choices were made), the total number of ways is
$$
n_1 \cdot n_2 \cdots n_r .
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
We prove the two-stage case directly; the general case then follows by repeating the
argument. List the possible first-stage choices as $f_1, f_2, \dots, f_a$ — there are $a$
of them by hypothesis. Fix one of them, say $f_i$. By hypothesis the second stage can then
occur in $b$ ways, which we list as $s_1, s_2, \dots, s_b$. A complete outcome of the whole
procedure is a *pair* $(f_i, s_j)$: which first choice, then which second choice. For each
of the $a$ choices of $f_i$, there are exactly $b$ pairs $(f_i, s_1), \dots, (f_i, s_b)$,
and no pair is counted under two different first choices (the pair records its own first
coordinate). So the complete outcomes split into $a$ groups, each of size $b$, with no
overlaps and nothing left out. Adding the group sizes,
$$
\underbrace{b + b + \cdots + b}_{a \text{ groups}} = a \cdot b ,
$$
which is the claim. For $r$ stages, group the first $r-1$ stages into a single "super-stage"
with $n_1 n_2 \cdots n_{r-1}$ ways (true for $r-1 = 2$ by what we just proved, and for larger
$r-1$ by repeating this same grouping), and pair it with the last stage's $n_r$ ways. The
two-stage result gives $(n_1 \cdots n_{r-1}) \cdot n_r = n_1 n_2 \cdots n_r$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Two cautions, because both are live in real problems and both are interview traps.

First, the phrase *"for each of those ways the second stage can occur in $b$ ways"* is
load-bearing: the *number* of second-stage options may not depend on which first choice was
made, even though the *identities* of the options can. Choosing a 2-letter code from the
alphabet with no repeats allowed: the first letter has $26$ options, and *whichever* you
pick, the second has $25$ (any letter but the one used) — the count $25$ is the same for
every first choice, so the principle applies and the answer is $26 \cdot 25$. The specific
forbidden letter changes; the count does not.

Second, if the count *does* change with the earlier choice, the simple product fails and
you must sum over cases instead. The multiplication principle is the engine of everything
below precisely because permutations and combinations are built so that the per-stage count
is stable.

That "sum over cases" move has a name and a one-line justification, and we will lean on it
in the Pascal, binomial-theorem, and multinomial proofs below, so we state it explicitly
now rather than smuggle it in later.

::: {#thm-addition}
**Theorem (addition principle).** If a finite collection of outcomes is partitioned into
$m$ *mutually exclusive* cases — no outcome belongs to two cases — with $a_1, a_2, \dots,
a_m$ outcomes in the respective cases, then the total number of outcomes is
$$
a_1 + a_2 + \cdots + a_m .
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
This is nothing more than the statement that the size of a union of *disjoint* finite sets
is the sum of their sizes. Let $A_1, \dots, A_m$ be the cases, regarded as sets of outcomes,
pairwise disjoint ($A_i \cap A_j = \varnothing$ for $i \ne j$) by the "mutually exclusive"
hypothesis. Counting the elements of $A_1 \cup \cdots \cup A_m$, every element lies in
exactly one $A_i$ (in *some* $A_i$ because the cases are exhaustive of the collection, in
*only one* because they are disjoint), so tallying the union element-by-element visits each
element once and lands it in precisely one of the per-case tallies $a_i = |A_i|$. The grand
tally is therefore $a_1 + \cdots + a_m$. This is exactly the finite, disjoint instance of
the additivity that [PROB-00] takes as an axiom for *probabilities* of disjoint events; here
we use it one level down, for the *sizes* of disjoint sets, which is its combinatorial
shadow. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Multiplication principle versus addition principle — when do you multiply and when do you
add?"* You **multiply** when you make a sequence of choices and want *all* of them ("first
this AND then that"): the stages compound. You **add** when an outcome falls into one of
several *mutually exclusive* cases and you want the total across cases ("either this OR
that, and they cannot both happen"): the disjoint cases accumulate. The cleanest tell:
*AND across stages → multiply; OR across disjoint cases → add.* This is exactly the
distinction between an intersection of independent stages and a union of disjoint events
from [PROB-00]'s event algebra — combinatorics is that algebra applied to *sizes* of sets.
:::

## Counting with and without replacement: ordered selections {#sec-ordered}

Now specialize the principle to the most common setup: drawing $k$ items, one at a time, in
order, from a pool of $n$ distinct items. There are two regimes, and they differ only in
whether a drawn item goes back.

**Ordered, with replacement.** After each draw you return the item, so every one of the $k$
draws faces the same $n$ options. By @thm-mult with $k$ stages each of size $n$,
$$
n \cdot n \cdots n \;(k \text{ factors}) \;=\; n^k .
$$ {#eq-with-repl}
This counts, e.g., the number of $k$-character strings over an $n$-symbol alphabet, or the
$6^3 = 216$ ordered outcomes of rolling a die three times.

**Ordered, without replacement.** Now a drawn item is *not* returned, so the pool shrinks by
one each draw. The first draw has $n$ options; whichever item it took, the second has $n-1$
remaining; the third has $n-2$; and the $k$-th has $n - (k-1) = n-k+1$. The per-stage count
drops by exactly one each time and — crucially — does not depend on *which* items were
removed, only on *how many*, so @thm-mult applies:
$$
n \,(n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-k+1) \;=\; \prod_{j=0}^{k-1}(n-j) .
$$ {#eq-falling}
This product of $k$ descending factors is the **falling factorial**; it counts the ordered
selections without replacement, which is precisely what we will name a permutation in
@sec-permutations.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
The single most common counting error is **using the wrong replacement regime**. "Three
people sit in three of ten chairs" is *without* replacement (a person cannot occupy two
chairs) → $10 \cdot 9 \cdot 8$. "A three-digit PIN" is *with* replacement (digits may
repeat) → $10^3$. Read the problem for whether an item, once used, is still available. The
arithmetic is unforgiving: $10 \cdot 9 \cdot 8 = 720$ versus $10^3 = 1000$ are different
answers to superficially similar questions.
:::

## The factorial: arranging all $n$ objects {#sec-factorial}

The most important special case of @eq-falling is $k = n$: arrange *all* $n$ distinct
objects in a row. Setting $k = n$, the descending product runs all the way down to its last
factor $n - n + 1 = 1$:
$$
n\,(n-1)(n-2)\cdots 2 \cdot 1 .
$$
This number has a name and a symbol.

::: {#def-factorial}
**Definition (factorial).** For a positive integer $n$, the **factorial** $n!$ ("$n$
factorial") is the product of every integer from $n$ down to $1$:
$$
n! \;=\; n\,(n-1)(n-2)\cdots 2 \cdot 1 \;=\; \prod_{j=1}^{n} j .
$$
By convention $0! = 1$.
:::

So $n!$ is exactly the number of distinct orderings (arrangements, **permutations**) of $n$
distinct objects: $n$ choices for the first slot, $n-1$ for the second, …, $1$ for the last,
multiplied by @thm-mult. For instance $3! = 6$ counts the six orderings of the letters
$\{A,B,C\}$: $ABC, ACB, BAC, BCA, CAB, CBA$. @fig-factorial-growth shows how violently $n!$
grows — faster than any exponential — which is *why* we need formulas rather than
enumeration the moment $n$ leaves the single digits.

Why define $0! = 1$? It is not arbitrary; it is the choice that makes every formula in this
article hold without special cases. The cleanest justification is the **empty-product**
convention: a product of *no* factors is $1$ (just as a sum of no terms is $0$), and
arranging zero objects can be done in exactly one way — the empty arrangement. We will see
in @sec-permutations and @sec-combinations that $0! = 1$ is precisely what makes
$P(n,n) = n!$ and $\binom{n}{0} = \binom{n}{n} = 1$ come out right; any other value would
break them.

```{python}
#| label: fig-factorial-growth
#| fig-cap: "Factorial growth, on a log scale. $n!$ outruns the exponential $2^n$ and even $10^n$ for modest $n$ — by $n=15$ there are over a trillion arrangements. This explosion is the practical reason combinatorics exists: enumeration is hopeless almost immediately, so we count with formulas instead. (Log scale, so straight lines are exponential growth; the factorial curves *upward* even here, growing faster than any fixed exponential.)"
ns = np.arange(1, 16)
fact = np.array([factorial(int(n)) for n in ns], dtype=float)
exp2 = 2.0 ** ns
exp10 = 10.0 ** ns

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(ns, fact, color=LINE, marker="o", ms=4, label=r"$n!$")
ax.plot(ns, exp10, color=ACCENT, marker="s", ms=4, label=r"$10^n$")
ax.plot(ns, exp2, color=POINT, marker="^", ms=4, label=r"$2^n$")
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("$n$"); ax.set_ylabel("count (log scale)")
ax.set_title("Factorial growth outruns any fixed exponential")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Permutations: ordered selections of $k$ from $n$ {#sec-permutations}

We now name @eq-falling and rewrite it with factorials, which is the form you will use.

::: {#def-permutation}
**Definition (permutation count).** The number of **permutations of $k$ objects taken from
$n$ distinct objects** — ordered selections without replacement — is written $P(n,k)$ (also
$_nP_k$ or $(n)_k$) and equals the falling factorial of @eq-falling:
$$
P(n,k) \;=\; n\,(n-1)\cdots(n-k+1) \;=\; \prod_{j=0}^{k-1}(n-j), \qquad 0 \le k \le n .
$$
:::

This is already a complete formula, but the factorial form is tidier and reveals structure.
Take the descending product $n(n-1)\cdots(n-k+1)$ and multiply *and divide* by the missing
tail $(n-k)(n-k-1)\cdots 1 = (n-k)!$. Multiplying the numerator by that tail completes it
into $n!$, and we have divided by the same tail to compensate:
$$
P(n,k)
= n(n-1)\cdots(n-k+1)
= \frac{n(n-1)\cdots(n-k+1)\,\cdot\,(n-k)(n-k-1)\cdots 1}{(n-k)(n-k-1)\cdots 1}
= \frac{n!}{(n-k)!}.
$$ {#eq-Pnk}

Every factor is accounted for: the numerator is the full $n!$, and the denominator $(n-k)!$
cancels exactly the factors from $(n-k)$ down to $1$ that we did *not* want, leaving the top
$k$ descending factors. Two sanity checks confirm the conventions earn their keep:

- $k = n$ (arrange everything): $P(n,n) = n!/(n-n)! = n!/0! = n!/1 = n!$, matching
  @sec-factorial. This is *why* $0! = 1$ is the right convention.
- $k = 0$ (select nothing): $P(n,0) = n!/n! = 1$ — there is exactly one way to choose nothing
  (the empty selection), and the formula agrees.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Compute the number of ways to award gold, silver, and bronze medals among 8 finalists."*
This is a permutation: the medals are *ordered* (gold ≠ silver), and a runner gets at most
one (no replacement). So $P(8,3) = 8!/5! = 8 \cdot 7 \cdot 6 = 336$. The reflex to train: the
instant you hear "first, second, third" or "in order" or "ranked," order matters and you are
in the permutation column. We will contrast this with the *unordered* version — "choose 3 of
8 to advance, no ranking" — in the very next section, and the factor between the two answers
will be exactly $3!$.
:::

## Combinations: when order does not matter {#sec-combinations}

Often we do not care about the order of a selection — only *which* items were chosen. A poker
hand is the same hand however you sort it; a committee of three is the same committee whoever
was named first. To count unordered selections, we use the permutation count and *correct for
overcounting*.

Here is the key idea, and it is the whole article in one move. Counting *ordered* selections
of $k$ items (that is $P(n,k)$) counts each *unordered* selection many times — once for every
ordering of its $k$ chosen items. By @sec-factorial, a set of $k$ distinct items has exactly
$k!$ orderings. So $P(n,k)$ has counted each unordered selection $k!$ times over. To get the
count of unordered selections, divide by $k!$:
$$
\binom{n}{k} \;=\; \frac{P(n,k)}{k!}.
$$ {#eq-comb-quotient}

We can make the overcounting argument completely rigorous with the multiplication principle,
so that "divide by $k!$" is a theorem, not a slogan.

::: {#thm-comb}
**Theorem (combination count).** The number of **combinations of $k$ objects taken from $n$
distinct objects** — unordered selections without replacement — written $\binom{n}{k}$ (read
"$n$ choose $k$"; also $_nC_k$ or $C(n,k)$) is
$$
\binom{n}{k} \;=\; \frac{n!}{k!\,(n-k)!}, \qquad 0 \le k \le n .
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
Build any *ordered* selection of $k$ from $n$ — there are $P(n,k)$ of them by
@def-permutation — as a two-stage procedure, then apply @thm-mult *in reverse* to solve for
the unknown count of unordered selections, which we name $C$.

- **Stage 1:** choose the *set* of $k$ items, ignoring order. Let the (unknown) number of
  ways be $C$ — this is exactly the quantity $\binom{n}{k}$ we want.
- **Stage 2:** arrange the chosen $k$ items into an order. By @sec-factorial there are
  exactly $k!$ orderings of $k$ distinct items, and this count is $k!$ *regardless of which
  $k$ items were chosen* — so @thm-mult applies.

Every ordered selection arises in exactly one way from this two-stage procedure (its set is
determined, and its ordering is determined), and conversely each (set, ordering) pair gives a
distinct ordered selection. So the two-stage count equals the ordered count:
$$
C \cdot k! \;=\; P(n,k) \;=\; \frac{n!}{(n-k)!},
$$
using @eq-Pnk for $P(n,k)$. Solving for $C$ by dividing both sides by $k!$,
$$
C \;=\; \binom{n}{k} \;=\; \frac{P(n,k)}{k!} \;=\; \frac{n!}{k!\,(n-k)!},
$$
which is both @eq-comb-quotient and the stated formula. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The factor of $k!$ between a permutation and a combination is the single most important
relationship in elementary counting, so let us see it concretely. @fig-perm-vs-comb lists,
for $n=4$ and $k=2$, the $P(4,2)=12$ ordered selections grouped into the
$\binom{4}{2}=6$ unordered ones — each unordered pair appears in exactly $2! = 2$ orders, and
$12 / 2 = 6$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-perm-vs-comb
#| fig-cap: 'Permutations vs. combinations for $n=4$ items $\{A,B,C,D\}$, $k=2$. The $P(4,2)=12$ *ordered* selections (left) collapse into $\binom{4}{2}=6$ *unordered* selections (right): each combination appears in exactly $2!=2$ orders, so dividing the ordered count by $2!$ gives the unordered count. This $\div k!$ is the whole content of @eq-comb-quotient.'
from itertools import permutations, combinations as itercomb
items = ["A", "B", "C", "D"]
perms = list(permutations(items, 2))
combs = list(itercomb(items, 2))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
ax.axis("off")
ax.text(0.16, 1.02, f"Ordered: P(4,2) = {len(perms)}", fontsize=11.5, ha="center", color=POINT)
ax.text(0.78, 1.02, f"Unordered: C(4,2) = {len(combs)}", fontsize=11.5, ha="center", color=ACCENT)

# group the 12 ordered pairs by their underlying set, 2 per combination
for row, c in enumerate(combs):
    y = 0.92 - row * 0.155
    ordered_versions = [p for p in perms if set(p) == set(c)]
    txt = "   ".join("".join(p) for p in ordered_versions)
    ax.text(0.16, y, txt, fontsize=12, ha="center", family="monospace", color=POINT)
    ax.annotate("", xy=(0.62, y), xytext=(0.34, y),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=GREY, lw=1.3))
    ax.text(0.78, y, "".join(c), fontsize=13, ha="center", family="monospace", color=ACCENT)
    ax.text(0.47, y + 0.045, r"$\div 2!$", fontsize=9, ha="center", color=GREY)
ax.set_xlim(0, 1); ax.set_ylim(0.0, 1.06)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"How many 5-card poker hands are there from a 52-card deck?"* Order does not matter — a hand
is a set — so this is a combination:
$$
\binom{52}{5} = \frac{52!}{5!\,47!}
= \frac{52 \cdot 51 \cdot 50 \cdot 49 \cdot 48}{5!}
= \frac{311{,}875{,}200}{120} = 2{,}598{,}960 .
$$
Notice the efficient way to compute it: write $\binom{n}{k} = P(n,k)/k!$ and only carry the
top $k$ descending factors $52 \cdot 51 \cdots 48$ over $5!$ — never form the full $52!$,
which has $68$ digits. Whether you are asked for ordered hands ($P(52,5)$, if "dealt in
order" mattered) or unordered hands ($\binom{52}{5}$) is the entire question; the factor
between them is $5! = 120$.
:::

## The symmetry $\binom{n}{k} = \binom{n}{n-k}$ {#sec-symmetry}

The combination count has a beautiful symmetry that is both a computational shortcut and a
piece of genuine insight.

::: {#thm-symmetry}
**Theorem (symmetry of the binomial coefficient).** For $0 \le k \le n$,
$$
\binom{n}{k} = \binom{n}{n-k}.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
*Algebraically*, substitute $n-k$ for $k$ in @thm-comb and watch the two factorials in the
denominator simply swap places:
$$
\binom{n}{n-k}
= \frac{n!}{(n-k)!\,\bigl(n-(n-k)\bigr)!}
= \frac{n!}{(n-k)!\,k!}
= \frac{n!}{k!\,(n-k)!}
= \binom{n}{k},
$$
since multiplication in the denominator is commutative, $(n-k)!\,k! = k!\,(n-k)!$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The algebra is one line, but the *meaning* is worth more than the proof. **Choosing which
$k$ items to include is the same act as choosing which $n-k$ items to exclude.** Every time
you pick a committee of $k$ from $n$ people, you simultaneously pick the $n-k$ people left
off it; the two choices are in perfect one-to-one correspondence, so they must be equinumerous.
This "bijection" argument — pair up the things you are counting with things you already know
how to count — is the deepest tool in combinatorics, and here it gives the symmetry for free,
with no factorials at all. Practically, it lets you replace a hard computation with an easy
one: $\binom{50}{48} = \binom{50}{2} = \frac{50 \cdot 49}{2} = 1225$, rather than grinding
through $48$ factors.

## Pascal's recurrence {#sec-pascal}

The next identity is the engine behind the whole structure of binomial coefficients and the
single most useful way to *build* them without factorials.

::: {#thm-pascal}
**Theorem (Pascal's recurrence).** For $1 \le k \le n-1$,
$$
\binom{n}{k} = \binom{n-1}{k-1} + \binom{n-1}{k}.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
We give the **combinatorial** proof — it explains *why* the identity is true, not just that
it is. Fix one particular item out of the $n$; call it the *special* item $\star$. We want to
count the $k$-subsets of the $n$ items, and we split them into two cases according to a
single yes/no question: *does the subset contain $\star$?* These two cases are mutually
exclusive (a subset either contains $\star$ or it does not) and exhaustive (every subset
falls into one), so by the addition principle (@thm-addition) the total is the sum of the two
case counts.

- **Case 1 — the subset contains $\star$.** Then $\star$ takes one of the $k$ slots, and we
  must choose the remaining $k-1$ items from the other $n-1$ (non-special) items. By
  @thm-comb that can be done in $\binom{n-1}{k-1}$ ways.
- **Case 2 — the subset does not contain $\star$.** Then all $k$ chosen items come from the
  other $n-1$ items, which can be done in $\binom{n-1}{k}$ ways.

Adding the two disjoint, exhaustive cases gives the total number of $k$-subsets,
$$
\binom{n}{k} = \binom{n-1}{k-1} + \binom{n-1}{k},
$$
as claimed. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Arranging these numbers in a triangle — row $n$ holding $\binom{n}{0}, \binom{n}{1}, \dots,
\binom{n}{n}$ — gives **Pascal's triangle**, in which @thm-pascal says *every interior entry
is the sum of the two entries above it*, and @thm-symmetry says *each row reads the same
left-to-right as right-to-left*. @fig-pascal draws it and verifies both identities visually.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pascal
#| fig-cap: 'Pascal''s triangle, rows $n=0$ through $7$. Each interior entry is the sum of the two directly above it (Pascal''s recurrence, @thm-pascal) — one such addition is highlighted: $\binom{6}{2}=15$ sits atop $\binom{7}{2}=21$. Each row is a palindrome (the symmetry $\binom{n}{k}=\binom{n}{n-k}$, @thm-symmetry). The row sums (right margin) are the powers of two, $\sum_k \binom{n}{k} = 2^n$, proved in @sec-binomial-theorem.'
N = 7
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.8))
ax.axis("off")
dx, dy = 0.62, 0.62
for n in range(N + 1):
    for k in range(n + 1):
        val = comb(n, k)
        x = (k - n / 2) * dx
        y = -n * dy
        highlight = (n in (6, 7)) and ((n == 6 and k in (1, 2)) or (n == 7 and k == 2))
        color = LINE if highlight else POINT
        ax.text(x, y, str(val), fontsize=11, ha="center", va="center",
                color="white", fontweight="bold",
                bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.28", fc=color, ec="none"))
    ax.text((n / 2) * dx + 0.9, -n * dy, f"$\\Sigma = {2**n}$",
            fontsize=10, va="center", color=GREY)
ax.set_xlim(-(N / 2) * dx - 0.6, (N / 2) * dx + 1.9)
ax.set_ylim(-N * dy - 0.5, 0.5)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why would you ever use Pascal's recurrence instead of the factorial formula?"* Two real
reasons. **Numerically:** the recurrence builds $\binom{n}{k}$ from smaller integers by
*addition only*, never forming the gigantic intermediate $n!$ that overflows or loses
precision — it is the basis of a stable, exact, integer dynamic-programming computation of
binomial coefficients. **Conceptually:** the "does it contain the special element?"
case-split in the proof is a reusable template. The same split derives the recurrences for
countless combinatorial quantities (the negative binomial's structure in [PROB-11], the
order-statistic counts in [PROB-23]). Knowing the recurrence is knowing the *method*, not
just the number.
:::

## The binomial theorem {#sec-binomial-theorem}

We now reach the result this whole article is built to deliver to the catalog: the **binomial
theorem**, which expands $(x+y)^n$ and explains why the coefficients are exactly the
combination counts $\binom{n}{k}$ — hence the name *binomial coefficient*.

::: {#thm-binomial}
**Theorem (binomial theorem).** For any real (or complex) numbers $x, y$ and any
non-negative integer $n$,
$$
(x + y)^n = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k}\, x^{k}\, y^{\,n-k}.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
Write the power as a product of $n$ identical factors:
$$
(x+y)^n = \underbrace{(x+y)(x+y)\cdots(x+y)}_{n \text{ factors}} .
$$
Expanding this product by the distributive law means: from *each* of the $n$ factors choose
either the $x$ term or the $y$ term, multiply the $n$ chosen terms together, and sum over
*every* possible way of choosing. That is precisely a sequence of $n$ independent two-way
choices — exactly the choice tree of @sec-intuition — so by @thm-mult there are $2^n$ such
selections, and the full expansion is the sum of those $2^n$ products before any combining.

Now collect like terms. A single product in that expansion contributes a factor of $x$ for
each factor where we picked $x$, and a factor of $y$ for each factor where we picked $y$. If
we picked $x$ from exactly $k$ of the $n$ factors (and therefore $y$ from the remaining
$n-k$), that product equals $x^k y^{\,n-k}$ — *the same monomial regardless of which $k$
factors supplied the $x$.* The $2^n$ products therefore partition into mutually exclusive
groups indexed by $k$ — the group "$x$ chosen from exactly $k$ factors" for each
$k = 0, 1, \dots, n$, every product landing in exactly one group — so by the addition
principle (@thm-addition) the number of products in group $k$ is the
**number of ways to choose which $k$ of the $n$ factors contribute the $x$.** Choosing $k$
factors out of $n$, order irrelevant, is counted by @thm-comb as $\binom{n}{k}$. All
$\binom{n}{k}$ products in group $k$ equal the same monomial $x^k y^{\,n-k}$, so the group
contributes $\binom{n}{k}\,x^k y^{\,n-k}$ to the sum. Summing the
collected groups over every $k$ from $0$ to $n$,
$$
(x+y)^n = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k}\, x^{k}\, y^{\,n-k},
$$
which is the theorem. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This proof is the reason the binomial coefficient is the bridge to probability. In [PROB-11]
the term $\binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}$ will be the probability of exactly $k$ successes in
$n$ independent trials — and the binomial theorem is exactly what guarantees those
probabilities sum to one. Set $x = p$ and $y = 1-p$:
$$
\sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k}\, p^{k}\,(1-p)^{n-k}
= \bigl(p + (1-p)\bigr)^{n} = 1^{n} = 1 .
$$ {#eq-binom-normalize}
A whole distribution normalizes to $1$ in a single line, *because* we counted its outcomes
with $\binom{n}{k}$. That is the payoff the catalog was waiting for.

One more identity falls out immediately and we promised it in @fig-pascal. Set $x = y = 1$ in
@thm-binomial:
$$
2^n = (1+1)^n = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k}\, 1^{k}\, 1^{\,n-k} = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k}.
$$ {#eq-row-sum}
The sum of each row of Pascal's triangle is $2^n$ — which is also obvious combinatorially: the
left side $2^n$ counts *all* subsets of an $n$-element set (each element is in or out, $n$
independent two-way choices, @sec-ordered), and the right side counts those same subsets
grouped by their size $k$. Two ways of counting the same thing must agree, and they do.

# The 2×2 table of sampling {#sec-2x2}

Every elementary counting problem in this article is one of four kinds, determined by two
independent yes/no questions:

1. **Does order matter?** (Is $AB$ different from $BA$?)
2. **Is replacement allowed?** (Can an item be chosen more than once?)

Two binary questions give four cells. Three of the four we have already derived; we complete
the table now with the fourth, which needs one new idea.

| | **Order matters** | **Order does not matter** |
|---|---|---|
| **With replacement** | $n^k$ (@eq-with-repl) | $\binom{n+k-1}{k}$ (@sec-stars-bars) |
| **Without replacement** | $\dfrac{n!}{(n-k)!} = P(n,k)$ (@eq-Pnk) | $\dfrac{n!}{k!\,(n-k)!} = \binom{n}{k}$ (@thm-comb) |

: The four ways to draw $k$ items from $n$ distinct items. Classifying a problem into the
right cell is the entire skill of elementary counting. {#tbl-2x2}

The three corners we know: ordered-with-replacement is the unrestricted product $n^k$;
ordered-without is the falling factorial $P(n,k)$; unordered-without is $\binom{n}{k}$. The
remaining corner — **unordered, with replacement** — counts *multisets*: selections of size
$k$ where repeats are allowed but order is ignored (e.g. how many ways to fill a bag with $k$
scoops from $n$ ice-cream flavors). It deserves its own short derivation because the naive
guesses ($n^k/k!$ or $\binom{n}{k}$) are both *wrong* — $n^k/k!$ is not even an integer in
general.

## Stars and bars: unordered with replacement {#sec-stars-bars}

::: {#thm-stars-bars}
**Theorem (multiset count / stars and bars).** The number of ways to choose $k$ items from $n$
distinct types, with repetition allowed and order ignored, is
$$
\binom{n+k-1}{k} = \binom{n+k-1}{n-1}.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
A multiset of size $k$ from $n$ types is fully described by *how many of each type* it
contains: a list of counts $(c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n)$ of non-negative integers with
$c_1 + c_2 + \cdots + c_n = k$ (we chose $c_i$ copies of type $i$, and the total is $k$). So
we need only count the number of such count-lists.

Encode each count-list as a row of symbols. Write $k$ **stars** $\star$ for the $k$ chosen
items and $n-1$ **bars** $\mid$ to partition them into $n$ groups (the bars are the $n-1$
fences between $n$ bins). For example, with $n=3$ types and $k=4$, the row
$\star\star \mid \;\mid \star\star$ means "two of type 1, zero of type 2, two of type 3."
*Every* arrangement of $k$ stars and $n-1$ bars in a row corresponds to exactly one valid
count-list (read off the number of stars in each of the $n$ regions the bars create), and
every count-list gives exactly one such arrangement — a perfect one-to-one correspondence.

So the count of multisets equals the count of these rows. A row has $k + (n-1)$ total
positions, and we need only choose *which* $k$ of them hold stars (the rest are bars) — an
unordered, without-replacement choice of positions, counted by @thm-comb:
$$
\binom{k + (n-1)}{k} = \binom{n+k-1}{k}.
$$
The alternative form $\binom{n+k-1}{n-1}$ follows by the symmetry @thm-symmetry (equivalently,
choose which $n-1$ positions hold the bars instead). $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

@fig-stars-bars illustrates the bijection for $n=3$, $k=4$, the heart of why the formula has
that shape.

```{python}
#| label: fig-stars-bars
#| fig-cap: 'Stars and bars for $n=3$ flavors, $k=4$ scoops. A multiset (how many of each flavor) is encoded as a row of $4$ stars and $n-1=2$ bars; choosing which of the $4+2=6$ positions are stars determines everything, so the count is $\binom{6}{4}=15$. Three example encodings are shown with the multiset they represent.'
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 3.4))
ax.axis("off")
examples = [
    ("★★|★|★", "(2, 1, 1)"),
    ("★★★★||", "(4, 0, 0)"),
    ("|★★|★★", "(0, 2, 2)"),
]
ax.text(0.30, 1.0, "encoding (stars & bars)", fontsize=11, ha="center", color=POINT)
ax.text(0.80, 1.0, "counts per flavor", fontsize=11, ha="center", color=ACCENT)
for row, (enc, cnt) in enumerate(examples):
    y = 0.78 - row * 0.24
    ax.text(0.30, y, enc, fontsize=18, ha="center", family="monospace", color=POINT)
    ax.annotate("", xy=(0.62, y), xytext=(0.46, y),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=GREY, lw=1.3))
    ax.text(0.80, y, cnt, fontsize=14, ha="center", family="monospace", color=ACCENT)
ax.text(0.5, 0.03,
        r"total $= \binom{n+k-1}{k} = \binom{6}{4} = 15$ multisets",
        fontsize=12.5, ha="center")
ax.set_xlim(0, 1); ax.set_ylim(0, 1.05)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"You're given a counting problem cold. What's your first move?"* Locate it in @tbl-2x2. Ask
the two questions in order: (1) *does swapping two chosen items give a different outcome?* —
if yes, order matters (top vs. bottom rows do **not** apply; you are in the "order matters"
column); (2) *can the same item be chosen twice?* — if yes, with replacement. Those two
answers pin the cell and hand you the formula. Misclassifying the cell is the only way to get
these problems wrong; the arithmetic afterward is mechanical. The dangerous cell is
**unordered-with-replacement** (stars and bars), because it is the one people have not
memorized and it does *not* equal any of the other three.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run the machinery on three honest problems. The first is the canonical equally-likely
calculation; the second is a real applied count; the third is deliberately placed at the
**edge of applicability**, where the equally-likely assumption that licenses all of this
quietly breaks.

## Example 1 — the birthday problem {#sec-birthday}

A staple of every probability course and interview, the **birthday problem** asks: in a room
of $m$ people, what is the probability that *at least two share a birthday*? It is the perfect
showcase because it is pure counting, the answer is famously counterintuitive, and the
equally-likely assumption is (nearly) honest here.

Assume $365$ days, each equally likely, and independent birthdays — so every one of the
$365^m$ ordered birthday-assignments is equally likely (ordered-with-replacement,
@eq-with-repl: each of the $m$ people independently lands on one of $365$ days). That is our
denominator. Computing the "at least two share" event directly is messy (two could share,
three could, two separate pairs could…), so we count the **complement** — *all $m$ birthdays
distinct* — and subtract from $1$. This is the classic move: when an event is a tangle of
cases but its complement is clean, count the complement (the rule $P(A) = 1 - P(A^c)$ is from
[PROB-00]).

All-distinct birthdays is an *ordered, without-replacement* assignment: the first person can
have any of $365$ days, the second any of the remaining $364$, …, the $m$-th any of
$365 - m + 1$. By @eq-Pnk that count is $P(365, m) = 365!/(365-m)!$. Dividing the favorable
(all-distinct) count by the total $365^m$,
$$
P(\text{all distinct}) = \frac{P(365,m)}{365^m} = \frac{365 \cdot 364 \cdots (365-m+1)}{365^m},
$$
and therefore
$$
P(\text{at least one shared birthday}) = 1 - \frac{365 \cdot 364 \cdots (365-m+1)}{365^m}.
$$ {#eq-birthday}

@fig-birthday plots @eq-birthday. The shock is that it crosses $50\%$ at just $m = 23$ people
and exceeds $99\%$ by $m = 57$ — far fewer than intuition (anchored on the $365$ days)
suggests. The reason is that the relevant count is *pairs* of people, $\binom{m}{2}$, which
grows quadratically: $23$ people already form $\binom{23}{2} = 253$ pairs, each a chance to
collide.

```{python}
#| label: fig-birthday
#| fig-cap: 'The birthday problem (@eq-birthday). The probability that at least two of $m$ people share a birthday crosses $50\%$ at only $m=23$ and exceeds $99\%$ by $m=57$. The counterintuitive speed comes from the *pairs* count $\binom{m}{2}$ (right axis annotation) growing quadratically — $23$ people already make $253$ pairs. Markers at $m=23$ and $m=57$.'
def p_shared(m):
    # 1 - P(all distinct), computed stably as a running product of ratios
    p_distinct = 1.0
    for i in range(m):
        p_distinct *= (365 - i) / 365.0
    return 1.0 - p_distinct

ms = np.arange(1, 81)
ps = np.array([p_shared(int(m)) for m in ms])

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(ms, ps, color=LINE)
ax.axhline(0.5, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.2)
for mm in (23, 57):
    ax.scatter([mm], [p_shared(mm)], color=ACCENT, s=70, zorder=4, edgecolor="white")
    ax.annotate(f"$m={mm}$\n$P={p_shared(mm):.3f}$", (mm, p_shared(mm)),
                textcoords="offset points", xytext=(8, -28 if mm == 23 else -2),
                fontsize=9, color=ACCENT)
ax.set_xlabel("number of people $m$"); ax.set_ylabel("P(at least one shared birthday)")
ax.set_title("23 people is enough for a coin-flip")
ax.set_ylim(0, 1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"P(shared) at m=23: {p_shared(23):.4f}")
print(f"P(shared) at m=57: {p_shared(57):.4f}")
print(f"pairs among 23 people: C(23,2) = {comb(23,2)}")
```

Every ingredient was a count from this article: the denominator $365^m$ from
ordered-with-replacement, the numerator $P(365,m)$ from ordered-without, the complement trick
from [PROB-00], and the quadratic intuition from $\binom{m}{2}$.

## Example 2 — the lottery, and why combinations make it hopeless {#sec-lottery}

A genuinely useful applied count: the odds in a $6/49$ lottery, where you choose $6$
distinct numbers from $1$ to $49$ and win the jackpot if your set matches the drawn set.
Order does not matter (the balls are sorted before comparison) and numbers are not repeated —
**unordered, without replacement**, the $\binom{n}{k}$ cell of @tbl-2x2:
$$
\binom{49}{6} = \frac{49!}{6!\,43!}
= \frac{49 \cdot 48 \cdot 47 \cdot 46 \cdot 45 \cdot 44}{6!}
= \frac{10{,}068{,}347{,}520}{720} = 13{,}983{,}816 .
$$
There are about $14$ million equally-likely tickets, so a single ticket wins with probability
$1/13{,}983{,}816 \approx 7.2 \times 10^{-8}$. This is an honest use of equally-likely
counting: a well-mixed lottery machine *designs in* the symmetry that makes every combination
equiprobable.

The article also lets us count **partial** matches — the smaller prizes for matching exactly
$j$ of the $6$ drawn numbers. This is a textbook use of the multiplication principle: to match
exactly $j$, choose $j$ of your numbers from the $6$ winning numbers **and** the other $6-j$
from the $43$ losing numbers, by @thm-mult:
$$
P(\text{match exactly } j) = \frac{\binom{6}{j}\binom{43}{6-j}}{\binom{49}{6}}, \qquad j = 0,1,\dots,6 .
$$ {#eq-hypergeom}
(This is the **hypergeometric** distribution; [PROB-11] names it and ties it to sampling
without replacement.) @fig-lottery plots these probabilities — the jackpot bar is so tiny it
is invisible against the near-certainty of matching only zero, one, or two numbers.

```{python}
#| label: fig-lottery
#| fig-cap: 'Lottery match probabilities (@eq-hypergeom) for a $6/49$ draw: probability of matching exactly $j$ of the $6$ drawn numbers with one ticket. The mass piles up at $j=0,1,2$ (you almost certainly match almost nothing); the jackpot $j=6$ has probability $\approx 7.2\times10^{-8}$ — invisible on the bar, annotated explicitly. Computed via the multiplication principle: $\binom{6}{j}$ ways to hit winners times $\binom{43}{6-j}$ ways to hit losers.'
total = comb(49, 6)
js = np.arange(0, 7)
probs = np.array([comb(6, j) * comb(43, 6 - j) / total for j in js])

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
bars = ax.bar(js, probs, color=POINT, width=0.6)
ax.set_xlabel("numbers matched, $j$"); ax.set_ylabel("probability")
ax.set_title("Lottery: you match almost nothing, almost always")
for j, p in zip(js, probs):
    if p > 0.01:
        ax.text(j, p + 0.008, f"{p:.3f}", ha="center", fontsize=9, color=POINT)
ax.annotate(f"jackpot $j=6$:\n$P \\approx {probs[6]:.1e}$",
            xy=(6, 0), xytext=(4.2, 0.30),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=LINE, lw=1.4),
            fontsize=9.5, color=LINE)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"C(49,6) = {total:,}")
print(f"P(jackpot) = 1 / {total:,} = {1/total:.3e}")
print("match-exactly-j probabilities:", np.round(probs, 6))
print("sum of probabilities (must be 1):", probs.sum())
```

The printed check that the seven probabilities sum to $1$ is the hypergeometric analogue of
the binomial normalization @eq-binom-normalize — another distribution made legitimate by
counting.

## Example 3 — the edge of applicability: when "equally likely" is a lie {#sec-edge}

Now the honest boundary. Every formula in this article computes a *size* — a number of
outcomes. Converting a size to a *probability* requires the outcomes to be **equally likely**,
and that assumption is doing enormous, often invisible, work. The edge case is any situation
where counting *looks* applicable but the outcomes are not interchangeable.

Take a concrete, real one: **the distribution of two-letter sequences (bigrams) in English
text.** A naive counting argument says: there are $26 \times 26 = 676$ ordered letter pairs
(ordered, with replacement — letters can repeat, and "TH" $\neq$ "HT"), so if we picked a
random bigram from text, each pair would have probability $1/676 \approx 0.00148$. That is
flatly, badly wrong, because letters in English are *not* equally likely and consecutive
letters are *not* independent: "TH", "HE", "IN" are wildly more common than "QZ" or "JX",
many of which never occur at all. @fig-bigram puts the counting prediction against reality.

```{python}
#| label: fig-bigram
#| fig-cap: "The edge of applicability. *Counting* says all $26^2=676$ letter bigrams are equally likely at $1/676$ (red line). *Reality* — bigram frequencies from a sample of English text — is violently unequal: a handful of bigrams (TH, HE, IN, ER, AN) dominate and most are rare or absent. Counting gives the right *denominator* (676 possible bigrams) but a wrong *probability*, because the equally-likely assumption that converts size into probability is false here. This is where the classical model stops and a probability *model* (PROB-11 onward) must take over."
# A short but real English passage; we tally adjacent-letter bigrams.
text = (
    "the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog and then the dog runs back "
    "to the other side of the field where the cattle and the horses graze in "
    "the morning light as the farmer brings the hay and water for them all "
    "and the children watch the animals from the fence near the great old barn"
)
letters = [c for c in text.lower() if c.isalpha()]
from collections import Counter
bigrams = Counter(a + b for a, b in zip(letters, letters[1:]))
total_bg = sum(bigrams.values())
top = bigrams.most_common(12)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.0))
labels = [b.upper() for b, _ in top]
freqs = [c / total_bg for _, c in top]
ax.bar(range(len(top)), freqs, color=POINT, width=0.7, label="observed frequency")
ax.axhline(1 / 676, color=LINE, lw=2.0, label=r"counting model: $1/676 \approx 0.0015$")
ax.set_xticks(range(len(top))); ax.set_xticklabels(labels, fontsize=9)
ax.set_xlabel("bigram"); ax.set_ylabel("relative frequency")
ax.set_title("Counting's equal-likelihood assumption fails on real text")
ax.legend(framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"distinct bigrams observed: {len(bigrams)} of 676 possible")
print(f"most common: {top[0][0].upper()} at {top[0][1]/total_bg:.3f}  vs  counting's {1/676:.4f}")
```

The lesson is not that counting is wrong — the denominator $676$ is exactly right, and if you
genuinely drew a uniformly random pair of letters (say, two independent fair $26$-sided dice
labeled A–Z) the $1/676$ would be exactly right too. The lesson is that **counting computes
probabilities only when a symmetry guarantees equal likelihood**, and you must check that
guarantee. A fair die, a shuffled deck, a designed lottery, a random permutation of treatment
labels — these *manufacture* the symmetry, and counting is exact. Natural data (letters,
words, incomes, arrival times) almost never has it, and there counting the outcomes is the
wrong tool; you need a probability model that assigns *unequal* probabilities, which is what
the rest of the catalog builds. Knowing which world you are in is the whole judgment.

# Failure modes and what to reach for instead {#sec-failure}

The machinery is mechanical once you are in the right cell of @tbl-2x2, so the failures are
failures of *classification* and of *assumption*, not of arithmetic.

**1. Wrong cell — order/replacement misread.** The dominant error. "How many ways to seat
$5$ people in $5$ chairs" is ordered-without ($5! = 120$); "how many $5$-person committees
from $20$" is unordered-without ($\binom{20}{5}$); "how many $5$-letter passwords" is
ordered-with ($26^5$). The fix is the two-question drill of @sec-2x2 — and when in doubt,
*enumerate a tiny case by hand* (as in @fig-perm-vs-comb) to see whether $AB$ and $BA$ are
being double-counted.

**2. The equally-likely assumption is false.** The edge case of @sec-edge. Counting sizes is
always valid; turning a size into a probability via "favorable / total" is valid **only**
under a genuine symmetry. When outcomes are not interchangeable, you have left this article's
domain and must reach for a probability *model* — the Bernoulli/binomial of [PROB-11], the
Poisson of [PROB-12], or any weighted distribution downstream.

**3. Forgetting indistinguishable items.** Our formulas assume the $n$ objects are
*distinct*. Arranging the letters of "MISSISSIPPI" is **not** $11!$, because the four S's are
identical and swapping two of them gives the same word. The correction is to divide by the
factorial of each repeat group — $\dfrac{11!}{4!\,4!\,2!\,1!}$ (four S, four I, two P, one M)
— which is the **multinomial coefficient**, the natural generalization of $\binom{n}{k}$ to
more than two groups. We build it next.

**4. The numbers explode.** $\binom{n}{k}$ and $n!$ overflow fast (@fig-factorial-growth). The
fixes: compute $\binom{n}{k}$ as the descending product $P(n,k)/k!$ (never the full $n!$); use
the symmetry @thm-symmetry to keep $k \le n/2$; or build with Pascal's recurrence (@thm-pascal)
for exact integers; or work with $\ln \binom{n}{k} = \ln n! - \ln k! - \ln(n-k)!$ via
`scipy.special.gammaln` when only the magnitude is needed.

## The multinomial coefficient {#sec-multinomial}

Failure mode 3 deserves a derivation, because the result is exactly the count that [PROB-23]
needs for order statistics and that [PROB-11] needs for the multinomial distribution.

::: {#thm-multinomial}
**Theorem (multinomial coefficient).** The number of distinct arrangements of $n$ objects of
which $n_1$ are of type 1, $n_2$ of type 2, …, $n_r$ of type $r$ (with
$n_1 + n_2 + \cdots + n_r = n$, objects of the same type indistinguishable) is
$$
\binom{n}{n_1, n_2, \dots, n_r} = \frac{n!}{n_1!\,n_2!\cdots n_r!}.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
First pretend all $n$ objects are distinct; then there are $n!$ arrangements by
@sec-factorial. But each *truly distinct* arrangement has been counted multiple times,
because permuting the $n_1$ type-1 objects among the positions they occupy does not change
the visible arrangement — and there are $n_1!$ such permutations (@sec-factorial). The same
holds independently for each type: $n_2!$ redundant orderings of the type-2 objects, and so
on. By the multiplication principle (@thm-mult), every visible arrangement has been counted
$n_1!\,n_2!\cdots n_r!$ times. Dividing the over-count $n!$ by the redundancy factor,
$$
\binom{n}{n_1,\dots,n_r} = \frac{n!}{n_1!\,n_2!\cdots n_r!}. \qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

The two-type case $r=2$, with groups of sizes $k$ and $n-k$, is exactly
$\dfrac{n!}{k!\,(n-k)!} = \binom{n}{k}$ — so the binomial coefficient is the multinomial
coefficient with two groups, and the "divide out the redundant orderings" argument here is
the same one that produced @thm-comb. The pattern is unmistakable: *count as if everything
were distinct, then divide by the orderings you cannot tell apart.* That single idea is the
spine of the whole article.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"How many distinct arrangements of the word STATISTICS are there?"* Ten letters with repeats
S(3), T(3), A(1), I(2), C(1). By @thm-multinomial,
$\dfrac{10!}{3!\,3!\,1!\,2!\,1!} = \dfrac{3{,}628{,}800}{6 \cdot 6 \cdot 2} = 50{,}400$.
The interviewer is checking that you (a) recognized indistinguishable letters and did *not*
say $10!$, and (b) divided by the factorial of *each* repeat group. Forgetting either is the
classic slip.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[PROB-00 *What Is Probability?*]**, which established the classical /
equally-likely interpretation — probability as favorable count over total count. This
article supplied the *counting* that interpretation needs and could not provide for itself.
From here the catalog continues, and several articles consume exactly what we built:

- **[PROB-11 *Bernoulli, Binomial & Negative-Binomial*]** — the immediate sequel. The
  binomial pmf $\binom{n}{k}p^k(1-p)^{n-k}$ uses $\binom{n}{k}$ to count the arrangements of
  $k$ successes among $n$ trials, and the binomial theorem (@thm-binomial, via
  @eq-binom-normalize) is precisely what makes it a valid distribution. The hypergeometric we
  met in @sec-lottery is named there too.
- **[PROB-23 *Order Statistics*]** — the density of the $k$-th smallest of $n$ samples is
  built from the multinomial count of @thm-multinomial: how many ways to split the sample
  into "below," "at," and "above" the order statistic.
- **Across tracks — Statistical Inference E3 (permutation tests)** — the null distribution of
  a permutation test is a uniform draw over the $n!$ relabelings of the data, the factorial
  of @sec-factorial; the test's validity rests on those relabelings being equally likely
  under the null, the same equal-likelihood premise that opened this article.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary source / origin.** The 1654 Pascal–Fermat correspondence on the *problem of
  points* (how to split the stakes of an interrupted game) is the founding document — and the
  first serious use of "probability = count of favorable / count of total." Pascal's
  *Traité du triangle arithmétique* (posthumous, 1665) is the source of Pascal's triangle and
  the recurrence of @thm-pascal.
- **The bijective method.** The symmetry proof (@thm-symmetry) and stars-and-bars
  (@thm-stars-bars) are your first taste of **bijective combinatorics** — proving two
  quantities equal by exhibiting a one-to-one correspondence rather than computing both. It is
  the most powerful and most elegant tool in the subject; Stanley's *Enumerative
  Combinatorics* is the canonical deep dive.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *What if the trials are not equally
  likely?* — that is the entire point of the distribution catalog beginning in [PROB-11].
  *What about counting that involves both inclusion and exclusion of overlapping conditions?*
  — that is the **inclusion–exclusion principle**, introduced in [PROB-00] for two events and
  generalized in advanced combinatorics. *Where does $\binom{n}{k}$ go for non-integer $n$?* —
  Newton's **generalized binomial theorem** extends @thm-binomial to real exponents via an
  infinite series, the gateway to the gamma function that [PROB-14] builds and that lets
  $\binom{\alpha}{k}$ make sense for any real $\alpha$.
